facts: Waves transfer energy from one place to another without transferring matter; for example, a wave on a rope moves energy along the rope but the rope itself only moves up and down.

The electromagnetic spectrum arranges waves by frequency and wavelength; radio waves have the lowest frequency and longest wavelength, while gamma rays have the highest frequency and shortest wavelength.
In an electric circuit, voltage is the energy per unit charge that pushes current through a conductor; a higher voltage across the same resistor results in a larger current.
Ohm's Law states that current (I) equals voltage (V) divided by resistance (R); if you double the voltage while keeping resistance constant, the current doubles.
Visible light is only a small part of the electromagnetic spectrum; it lies between infrared and ultraviolet, and different colors correspond to different frequencies within that range.
Magnetism and electricity are linked: a changing magnetic field can induce an electric current in a wire, and an electric current creates a magnetic field around the wire.

The speed of all electromagnetic waves in a vacuum is the same (about 300,000 km/s), regardless of their frequency or wavelength; this means that radio waves and X-rays travel at the same speed in empty space.
misconceptions: Students often think that waves carry matter from one place to another, believing that a water wave moves water particles across the ocean, when in fact water particles mostly move in circles and the wave energy travels.

Students may believe that higher frequency electromagnetic waves (like X-rays) travel faster than lower frequency ones (like radio waves), because they think higher energy means faster speed, but in vacuum all EM waves travel at the same speed.

Students often think that a higher voltage always means a higher current, ignoring that current also depends on resistance; if resistance increases, current may not increase even if voltage is higher.
